intro:
The book of Job names no author and gives no explicit date, so its composition has always been a matter of tradition and inference rather than record. It stands among the wisdom writings as an anonymous masterpiece exploring innocent suffering and the justice of God.

Ancient Jewish tradition (the Talmud, Baba Bathra) credited Moses with the book; others have proposed Job himself, his friend Elihu, Solomon, Hezekiah's men, or simply an unknown sage of Israel's wisdom tradition. None of these can be established, and the book's own silence about its author appears deliberate.

sections.title: The question of date
sections.body: The story is set in the patriarchal age: Job's wealth is reckoned in livestock, he acts as priest for his family, there is no mention of the Mosaic law, temple, or covenant nation, and lifespans are long. These features suggest a very early setting. Yet the polished poetry and theological depth lead many to date the book's writing later — anywhere from the age of Solomon to the exile — by an author retelling an ancient account.

sections.title: A real man, remembered
sections.body: Whoever composed it, Scripture treats Job as a genuine historical person: Ezekiel ranks him with Noah and Daniel as a byword for righteousness, and James holds up ‘the patience of Job’ as an example for the church. The book's anonymity thus attaches to its writer, not its subject.
